params.text:
Good records protect everyone

The Mental Capacity Act runs on evidence. If a decision about someone’s care is ever questioned, your records show that you followed the principles. Vague notes leave the person unprotected and leave you exposed.

  • Record the support you gave: the steps you took to help the person make the decision themselves.
  • Note the specific decision: what was being decided, and when.
  • Show the reasoning: for a best-interests decision, who was consulted and what the person’s own wishes and feelings were.
  • Write promptly and factually: stick to what you actually saw and heard.

Putting it together: Grace’s tablets

Grace has early dementia. This morning she refuses her tablets, insisting she has ‘already had them’. You know she has not. Rather than pressing her or slipping the tablets into her tea, you presume capacity and try to help her decide: you explain calmly what the tablets are for and offer them again after breakfast.

Later, when she is more settled, Grace takes them willingly. You record what happened, the support you gave, and that her capacity for this decision appeared to fluctuate through the morning.

params.question: In the scenario, why was it right to offer Grace her tablets again after breakfast rather than hiding them in her tea?

answers.text: It respected her right to decide, allowed for her fluctuating capacity, and avoided covertly medicating her without lawful authority.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Covert medication needs a capacity assessment and a formal best-interests process; presuming capacity and trying again is the right first step.

answers.text: Hiding tablets in food is quicker, so it was simply a matter of saving time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Speed is not the issue; covert administration without proper authority would be unlawful.

answers.text: Because staff can never give medication to a person with dementia under any circumstances.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. People with dementia can and do take medication; the point is to seek consent and support the decision.

answers.text: Because once Grace refused, the tablets could not be offered again that day.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A refusal in one moment does not close the door; offering again later respects fluctuating capacity.
